retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Iron profile: This profile includes serum iron, ferritin, and total iron-binding capacity, which help assess iron stores and iron utilization.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Hereditary Hemochromatosis -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: The serum ferritin concentration and serum transferrin saturation (TSAT) are increased in patients with HH. Further testing should be considered if the ferritin levels exceed 200 µg/L in menstruating females or 300 µg/L in males or nonmenstruating females, or if the TSAT is >45%. [1] TIBC values >450 mcg/dL (80.55 mmol/L) can also help diagnose pathological iron accumulation. [25] The negative predictive value for iron overload is 97% when serum ferritin is normal and TSAT <45%. [26]

retrieved_docs.content: Soluble transferrin receptor: This test differentiates between IDA and anemia of chronic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The following reference ranges for iron metabolism parameters valuable guidance for assessing iron status and diagnosing related disorders. Iron-binding capacity: 255 to 450 mcg/dL. Transferrin-iron saturation percentage: 25% to 35%. UIBC: normal values for UIBC may vary among laboratories, but most laboratories define their normal range as 111 to 343 mcg/dL. TIBC: normal values vary among laboratories, generally 240 to 450 mcg/dL. [22] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscle Cramps -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Serum ferritin and total iron-binding capacity: Patients with suspected restless leg syndrome, anemia, or pregnancy (ferritin only)

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of iron overload begins with low-cost, noninvasive laboratory testing. Serum iron levels are not reliable for chronic overload diagnosis. However, serum ferritin and transferrin saturation are widely used. Ferritin levels greater than 300 ng/mL in men and greater than 150 to 200 ng/mL in menstruating women raise suspicion of iron overload. However, ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and may be falsely elevated in inflammatory, infectious, or hepatic conditions, limiting its accuracy in reflecting the total body iron burden. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Anemia -- Evaluation -- Differentiation of microcytic anemias based on iron studies
retrieved_docs.content: Iron deficiency anemia: Low serum iron, high TIBC, and low ferritin. Anemia of chronic disease: Low serum iron, low TIBC, and high ferritin. Sideroblastic anemia: High serum iron, normal TIBC, and high ferritin. Thalassemia: Normal serum iron, normal TIBC, and normal ferritin.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Iron Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Normal or slightly increased ferritin levels are present in thalassemia. Transferrin levels are almost normal in thalassemia as compared to iron deficiency anemia. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Iron studies: Serum ferritin and transferrin levels can differentiate thalassemia from IDA. A normal or slightly increased ferritin level with nearly normal transferrin is more consistent with thalassemia than IDA, which often manifests with low ferritin and elevated transferrin.

retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Disease and Gonadal Failure -- Evaluation -- Other Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Iron studies Serum iron, total iron-binding capacity, and ferritin to assess iron status and rule out iron deficiency anemia or hemochromatosis, which can affect overall health and potentially impact gonadal function.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: TSAT is calculated as the ratio of serum iron to TIBC. It is one of the earliest biomarkers of iron deficiency and is useful when serum ferritin is unequivocal. A decrease (less than 20%) indicates an iron deficiency, either absolute or functional. [15] [3] Hemoglobin does not become low until a significant percentage of body iron becomes depleted. Hence, normal hemoglobin does not exclude iron deficiency. Other iron studies available for the evaluation of iron deficiency are:

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Function -- Second-Trimester Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of ≥2 abnormal results establishes the diagnosis of gestational diabetes. [16] See StatPearls' companion reference, " Gestational Diabetes ," for more information. A repeat complete blood count should also be performed when the gestational diabetes screen specimen is collected to screen for anemia, which is prevalent in pregnant women due to increased iron and folate requirements. Because iron deficiency is a common underlying etiology for anemia, iron studies should be performed in patients with abnormal results (see Table. Recommended Initial Prenatal Serum Laboratory Studies). [17] In patients with known Rhesus (Rh)-negative status with a negative antibody screen on initial prenatal testing, an antibody screen should be repeated at 28 weeks gestation. Anti(D)-immune globulin should also be administered at 28 weeks in these patients. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Deficiency Anemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: The United States Preventive Services Task Force has concluded that the evidence is not sufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for iron deficiency anemia in pregnant women and children ages 6 to 24 months. [12] [Level V]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Hereditary Hemochromatosis -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Total serum iron and total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) assays can be used in conjunction with ferritin assays to aid in diagnosing iron deficiency or overload. No significant differences in apparent concentration have been reported between serum and plasma samples for total iron determination.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Men and non-menstruating women: 8 mg Menstruating women: 18 mg Pregnancy: 27 mg Generally, the total transferrin in the blood is only 33% saturated. The total transferrin saturation falls to 16% or less during iron-deficient states. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Supplementation -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In the early stages, anemia may be present on routine bloodwork. Initial testing includes a complete blood count (CBC) with differential, which includes values such as WBC count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, RBC count, RBC indices. A reticulocyte count is helpful, as well.  If asymptomatic, the patient can be monitored with yearly CBC to check for any changes.  If symptomatic, more aggressive methods to identify the underlying cause are necessary. If iron deficiency is suspected, these patients should have iron studies assessed to check for serum iron, total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), transferrin saturation, and ferritin levels to determine if it is iron deficiency anemia, the most likely cause of microcytic anemia vs. other etiologies. Other tests that the clinician can order are hemosiderin and hepcidin, but are not necessary. Once the iron-deficiency is confirmed, then iron supplementation may be started. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2661
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy represent a broad category of maternal health issues that include specific conditions such as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before birth), خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy), and فقر الدم في أثناء الحمل (anemia during pregnancy). Recognizing these sub‑conditions under the umbrella of other complications of pregnancy is important for comprehensive prenatal assessment, as each can affect fetal development and maternal outcomes. Effective screening and management of drug dependence, thyroid abnormalities, and anemia are essential components of obstetric care to reduce risks to both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.
